content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 12, 1991


Terry C. Andrus
Deputy County Counsel
Offices of the County Counsel
County of Orange
10 Civic Center Plaza
P.O. Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA 92702-1379

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-080

Dear Mr. Andrus:

You have requested assistance concerning the new provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ limiting gifts and
honoraria. Since you have not named a specific elected official
on whose behalf you have requested this advice, we provide the
following informal response to your question.²

QUESTION

Are payments to a local elected official in connection with a
speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or
like gathering gifts subject to the new gift and honoraria limits
of the Act?

CONCLUSION

Reimbursement for actual travel expenses and reasonable
subsistence in connection with a speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering is exempted from

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-080
Page 2


the $1,000 limit on gifts and honoraria applicable to local
elected officials.

However, absent some express exception in the Act, all public
officials must disclose the name and address of each source of
gifts and honoraria of $50 or more in value, the amount and the
date on which the gift was received, and a general description of
the business activity of the donor. Moreover, a public official
may not make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
which will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on any donor of, any
intermediary or agent for a donor of, gifts or honoraria
aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.

DISCUSSION

Section 89501 provides:

No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering, or any gifts, from any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000), in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.

Based on this section local elected officers are now subject
to a $1,000 aggregate limit for gifts and honoraria received from
any single source. However, the statute specifically excludes
from the $1,000 limit reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection with a speech given, article
published, or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.3

Please note, however, absent some express exception in the
Act, all public officials must disclose the name and address of
each source of gifts of $50 or more in value, the amount and the
date on which the gift was received, and a general description of


3 since the exceptions for travel in Section 89506 are
necessarily included within the broader exception in Section
89501, we are advising that Section 89506 is not applicable to
local elected officers. This is an issue which may be the subject
of future Commission regulation.

Our File No. I-91-080
Page 3


the business activity of the donor.⁴ (Section 87207.) Moreover,
public officials may not make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision which will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any
donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or
gifts aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made. (Sections 87100, et seq.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


⁴ Honoraria received may be disclosed on a separate honoraria
schedule established by the Commission or shall be reported as a
gift. (Regulation 18728(b).) If it is clear from all of the
surrounding circumstances that the services provided to the source
of the payment was equal or greater consideration than the payment
received, the honorarium may be disclosed as income. However, the
public official has the burden of proving that the consideration
was of equal or greater value, unless the public official is a
defendant in a criminal action. (Regulation 18728(c).)

⁵ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
